description: External secp256k1 research signal classes mapped to current repository evidence and expected handling.

classes.reason: Recent GLV/ZVP papers land in an already tracked exploit class with deterministic repo coverage. New papers still matter, but they usually expand an existing threat line instead of introducing an unmapped class.
Nonce timing leakage and noisy-HNP follow-up work already map to direct regression probes in the audit tree.
DVFS-style timing leakage covered by two complementary tests: timing uniformity regression (hertzbleed_dvfs_timing) and scalar Hamming-weight blinding verification (hertzbleed_scalar_blind).
Sub-bit nonce leakage already has a deterministic audit probe in the repository.
RFC 9591 invariants and adversarial threshold-signing cases already exist as deterministic repo checks.
Standard Schnorr and ECDSA batch verification already have correctness and poisoning regression coverage.

keywords: private key can be extracted on signing
classes.reason: The malicious-object and type-confusion signing class is already tracked by a dedicated exploit-style regression probe.
Non-canonical or out-of-range ECDSA `(r,s)` acceptance is already covered by explicit strict-parse and verification regression probes.
Research on modified ECDSA batch verification is interesting, but it targets non-standard signature variants rather than the standard compatibility surface this repository currently ships.

Dual-prover formal verification: (1) Z3 SMT — 7 theorems, 17 proofs (GCD preservation, det invariant, zeta bound, g-convergence, CT mask correctness, 590-step sufficiency, CT≡branching equivalence); (2) Lean 4 — 19 theorems via native_decide (g_sum_even, absorbing state, zeta bounds, 9 computational 590-step witnesses incl. G.x/G.y, 5 CT mask proofs, full CT≡branching equivalence at 8-bit exhaustive). Runtime PoC SGD-1..11 also present. CI gate runs both provers on every push.
This class is not a security gap by itself, but fresh M-ary/precompute results may be worth benchmarking against the current precompute and multiscalar pipeline.
Hardware-wallet architecture work is worth watching, but it does not directly map to a missing software-library assurance surface unless the hardware roadmap changes.
ePrint 2025/705 shows algebraic key recovery when two nonces have an affine relation. RFC 6979 deterministic nonces are immune. PoC validates both the attack and the library's immunity.
ePrint 2023/841 documents real-world Bitcoin key recovery when nonces are composed from hash and key halves. RFC 6979 is immune by design. PoC validates the attack vector and immunity.
CVE-2024-31497 (PuTTY) and CVE-2024-1544 (wolfSSL) show key recovery from nonce bias caused by oversized random mod n. RFC 6979 HMAC-DRBG is immune. PoC validates the bias mechanism and immunity.
ePrint 2017/975 shows key recovery when faults are injected during deterministic nonce generation. PoC validates the attack mechanism and confirms the library's deterministic nonce path consistency.

ePrint 2024/1380 documents a 14-year Infineon vulnerability where non-CT Extended Euclidean Algorithm for modular inversion leaked key material via EM side-channel. PoC verifies SafeGCD and Fermat paths produce correct and consistent results across pathological inputs.

ePrint 2025/654 shows cascading key compromise when multiple keys share the same nonce via weak PRNG. One compromised key reveals all others. RFC 6979 derives nonces from (key, msg) making cross-key sharing impossible. PoC validates the attack and RFC 6979 immunity.

ePrint 2025/1846 proves that hash input ordering in Fiat-Shamir schemes affects multi-user security. BIP-340 uses R||P||msg (correct: commitment before message). PoC verifies all 4 wrong orderings produce different challenges and fail verification.

ePrint 2025/076 extends classical ZVP to the Dependent Coordinates Problem on GLV multiscalar multiplication, recovering ~63% of key bits. PoC validates r-value distribution, β-endomorphism probing resilience, and static-key stability.

ePrint 2024/296 demonstrates lattice sieving with interval reduction that breaks ECDSA with <1 bit nonce leakage. PoC validates bit distribution uniformity, low-s normalisation, and chi-squared test on MSB buckets.

ePrint 2017/975 shows deterministic nonces (RFC 6979) are vulnerable to DFA: two faulted signatures with same message yield differential that reveals the key. PoC validates triple determinism, high Hamming distance under 1-bit fault, and Schnorr determinism.

CVE-2024-49364: tiny-secp256k1 type confusion allows k-reuse → key extraction. CVE-2024-49365: verify() false positive. CVE-2022-41340: missing r,s range validation. PoC validates k-uniqueness across 1024 signatures, boundary r/s rejection, and false-positive resistance.

ePrint 2020/945 breaks blind Schnorr in concurrent sessions via polynomial-time ROS solver. PoC validates 256-session nonce independence, algebraic forgery rejection, batch corruption detection, and uniform error codes.

ePrint 2026/075 shows FROST2 has TS-SUF-2 not TS-SUF-4 security (weak binding). ePrint 2025/1001 attacks adaptive security of threshold Schnorr. PoC validates session nonce uniqueness, key tweak resilience, taproot binding, and batch verify with mixed keys.

ePrint 2024/589 + 2025/935: Blind-Folded SPA exploits cmov/cswap power leakage even without template profiling. PoC validates ECDSA/ECDH/Schnorr determinism, HW-extreme keys timing within 3x ratio, and multi-sign consistency.

ePrint 2025/1293: ECTester systematic point validation testing found widespread ECC library failures — infinity acceptance, off-curve acceptance, twist-point acceptance. PoC validates all rejection paths + negate/tweak/combine edge cases.

ePrint 2025/306 + 2025/1353: Dimensional eROS attack + Benhamouda-Zottarel blind sig forgery exploit concurrent session nonce correlation. PoC validates 256-session r-value uniqueness, batch verify, and Hamming distance entropy for nonce bias detection.

ePrint 2026/663: Modified ECDSA batch verification with weak randomization allows forgery injection. PoC validates corruption detection, invalid-position pinpointing, wrong pk/msg rejection, and batch scaling from empty to 1024 entries.

ePrint 2025/1151 (Suchánek, Sýs, Chmielewski): 3D GLV decomposition for Schnorr verification. EVALUATED — NOT APPLICABLE to UltrafastSecp256k1 for the following reasons: (1) secp256k1 λ² ≡ -λ-1 (mod n) causes the 3D lattice to degenerate to 2D (the (1,1,1) vector collapses one dimension). LLL reduction confirms: reduced basis has 128-bit vectors, not 85-bit. (2) Joint 3D lattice over two independent scalars (s, -e) gives ~170-bit scalars (WORSE than 4D 128-bit by Minkowski bound). (3) Our implementation already uses w=15 precomputed generator tables (8192 entries), making the generator scalar nearly free — the bottleneck is the P-side 2D GLV at 128 doublings, which 3D cannot reduce. (4) No public reference implementation exists. Full paper PDF inaccessible for algorithm reconstruction. Current schnorr_verify: 22-26 us/op baseline (10K ops). Re-evaluate if full paper becomes available or if the authors publish a secp256k1 implementation.

ePrint 2024/1225 Dark Skippy hardware-wallet nonce exfiltration: ufsecp uses RFC 6979 deterministic nonce with no external nonce-callback injection surface. Verified: determinism, r-value uniformity, Schnorr aux_rand independence, 128-sig HW uniformity.

Transient execution attacks (Spectre-class) on RISC-V cores can bypass code-discipline CT guarantees — the same class of leak that CAAS caught via compiler optimization on the RISC-V port. Current CT coverage uses dudect + LLVM MemorySanitizer; neither catches speculative-load leakage. A dedicated audit surface (e.g., hardware PMU probe or microarch simulator) is needed to fully close this vector on RISC-V.

ePrint 2022/411 Frozen Heart: ZK Fiat-Shamir transcript must bind all public parameters. Verified: knowledge-proof pubkey/msg binding, DLEQ generator/point binding, Bulletproof commitment binding. All statement-swap and corruption tests return error. Distinct from fiat_shamir_hash_order (ePrint 2025/1846) which covers hash function ORDER confusion rather than statement OMISSION.
